changes_made: Renamed 'k8s_rbac_user_access_review' to 'k8s_rbac_user_access_audit' for consistency with audit terminology
review_notes: The control 8.2.6 is applicable to Kubernetes as it involves managing user accounts and ensuring inactive accounts are addressed, which can be mapped to RBAC and audit logging in Kubernetes. The original function names were mostly clear but needed slight adjustments for clarity and consistency. The function 'k8s_rbac_inactive_user_cleanup' was renamed to specify 'service account' as Kubernetes primarily deals with service accounts rather than traditional user accounts. The function 'k8s_rbac_user_access_review' was renamed to 'k8s_rbac_user_access_audit' to align with audit terminology, which is more appropriate for reviewing access.
naming_improvements: Specify 'service account' in function names when applicable
Use 'audit' instead of 'review' for consistency with audit logging terminology
title: Examine user accounts and last logon information, and interview personnel to verify that any inac...
step1_reasoning: The control 8.2.6 focuses on examining user accounts and last logon information to verify inactive accounts are addressed. In Kubernetes, this can be mapped to ensuring that RBAC configurations are regularly reviewed to identify and clean up inactive users or service accounts. Additionally, audit logging can be configured to track user activities, which helps in identifying inactive accounts based on lack of activity.
